Confirmed NICHARG=4 (ALCOMN.MAC:813) is a separate constant (physical charge-array allocation, NCHARG=NPCHARG+NICHARG at 814) distinct from the dynamic WCHAMX+1 cap used by FIREIC — the claim correctly distinguishes 'physical slot count' from 'live cap'. On our side confirmed MAX_ENEMY_BULLETS=4 (rules.ts:62) and sim.ts:225 ('if (s.enemyBullets.length >= MAX_ENEMY_BULLETS) break // hard cap') applied flatly at every level, plus confirmed enemyFireChance is indexed by s.enemyBullets.length (sim.ts:229), so the claimed knock-on effect on effective fire rate is real and directly traceable in the code. No issues found.

title: Enemy bolt speed is invader speed + 2.0 along-units/frame (0.254 depth/s), not +0.72 depth/s — and not the 0.536 this finding first prescribed
claim: TCHARIN is a single TB record for waves 1-99 with byte -64, and TB (DOTB, 829-832) means 'byte + WINVIL'. So WCHARL = WINVIL - 64 for EVERY wave, then TIMES8 scales it: the bolt is always exactly 64*8/256 = 2.0 along-units/frame faster than the invader that fired it. At the ROM's real 28.44 fps (FR-001) that offset is 2.0 * (256/9) / 224 = 0.254 depth/s, and the wave-1 bolt's absolute speed is (44+64)/32 = 3.375 along/frame = 0.4286 depth/s. Our ENEMY_BOLT_SPEED_OFFSET is 0.72 depth/s — 2.83x the ROM's offset — giving 0.368 + 0.72 = 1.088 depth/s at L1, 2.54x the arcade's bolt. The code comment claiming 'flipper-relative +0xc0' is not what the source says.
reasoning: Bolt speed is the whole dodge window, and ours is more than twice the arcade's. Corrected for the 28.444 fps rebase (see FR-010 in pair-11-framerate-adjudication.json): the CLASS stands, but the fix value this finding originally prescribed — 0.536 — is itself 2.0 * 60/224, the same invented base, and applying it as written would bake the 2.11x error permanently into the code and then 'confirm' it against this audit. The correct constant is 0.254 depth/s (= 2.0 * ROM_FPS / 224); once the flipper speed is also rebased (FR-008) the L1 bolt lands on the ROM's 0.4286 depth/s exactly, and the offset stays correct at every level because it is additive in both models. The rebase must land BEFORE this fix. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC. TB is decoded correctly: CONTOUR's DOTB (829-832) is literally 'LDA NY,TEMP3 / CLC / ADC WINVIL / RTS' — byte + WINVIL, exactly as claimed. TCHARIN (600-601) is a single TB record spanning waves 1-99 with byte -64 (=0xC0 signed, matching our code's own comment '+0xc0'), so WCHARL_raw = WINVIL_raw - 64 on every wave, before either is run through TIMES8 (560-578, verified by hand: three ASL/ROL pairs = x8, 16-bit result with high byte = along-units integer, low byte = /256 fraction). Traced the actual consumer: MOVCHA's enemy-charge branch (2555-2568, 'TOWARD PLAYER') adds WCHARL to CHARYL and WCHARIN to CHARY every frame — this is genuinely the governing bolt-movement code, not dead or overridden. Since both WCHARL and WINVIL get the identical x8 scale, the constant -64 raw delta becomes exactly 64*8/256 = 2.0 along-units/frame regardless of wave — verified WINVIL(wave1)=-44 via TINVIN's TA record (592), giving WCHARL_raw(wave1)=-108, scaled = -3.375 along/frame, vs WINVIL scaled = -1.375 along/frame; difference = 2.0 exactly, matching the claim's '64+44)/32=3.375' arithmetic. Attacked on unit grounds: the claim explicitly rejects its own prior 60 Hz-based fix (0.536 = 2.0*60/224) in favor of 0.254 = 2.0*(256/9)/224, correctly using the adjudicated 28.444 fps ground truth — this survives. Our code's ENEMY_BOLT_SPEED_OFFSET=0.72 (rules.ts:72) is neither 0.254 nor 0.536, so it is wrong under any frame-rate reading. Holds.

title: The enemy-fire depth gate is 'not too near the rim' — there is no far-end minimum depth at all

ours.verbatim: export const ENEMY_FIRE_MIN_DEPTH = 0x30 / 0x100   // ≈ 0.188 of the well

claim: FIREIC's only positional gate is INVAY >= ILINLIY+$20 = $30 (2694-2695, 'INVADER LOW ENOUGH?'), i.e. the invader must be at least $20 units BELOW the rim. In our depth convention (depth = ($F0 - Y)/224) that is depth <= (240-48)/224 = 0.857. There is NO minimum-distance-from-the-far-end check: an invader that hatched one frame ago at the bottom of the well may fire immediately if its INVACT timer is up. We enforce BOTH an invented far-end floor (ENEMY_FIRE_MIN_DEPTH = 0.188, sim.ts:227) and a near cap of 0.9 rather than 0.857. FIREIC also refuses to fire while the invader is mid-jump (INVMOT set, 2702-2704) — we have no such rule.
reasoning: Our far-end floor silences every freshly-spawned enemy for the first 19% of its climb, which the arcade does not do; and our near cap lets enemies shoot from 0.857-0.9 where the arcade has already stopped them. Both are single-constant changes; the mid-jump rule is one condition in stepEnemyFire. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:2687-2729 (FIREIC, the SBTTL-labelled 'FIRE INVADER CHARGE' routine — the governing code, not a decoy). Confirmed ILINLIY=0x10 (ALCOMN.MAC:820, no trailing period = hex per this assembler's radix convention) and the literal '20' at 2694 is likewise hex (0x20=32), so ILINLIY+20 = 0x30 = 48 decimal, matching the claim exactly. Traced the Y-direction convention independently via INICUR (ALWELG.MAC:296-297, CURSY=ILINLIY=0x10 at rest) and JFUSEUP's climb-up branch (2098-2108, which ADDS a negative WFUSIH to INVAY while climbing 'up', and clamps only once INVAY drops below CURSY) — this proves Y DECREASES toward the rim, so the depth=(0xf0-Y)/224 convention our own code uses elsewhere (rules.ts SPIKER_TURNAROUND_DEPTH, PULSAR_NEAR_FAR_DEPTH) is the right direction, and INVAY>=0x30 does correctly gate 'not within the last 32 Y-units of the rim' (depth<=0.857), with no far-end/floor counterpart — the single CMP/IFCS at 2694-2695 is the ONLY positional test in the routine. Verified the mid-jump gate too: 'LDA X,INVAC1 / AND I,INVMOT / IFEQ' (2702-2704) with INVMOT=0x80 (ALCOMN.MAC:852) — only fires when the motion bit is clear, confirming invaders mid-jump can't fire. Checked our code: ENEMY_FIRE_MIN_DEPTH = 0x30/0x100 ≈0.188 (rules.ts:65) is a genuinely invented, wrongly-scaled (divides by 256 not 224) and wrongly-directioned floor with no ROM counterpart, and ENEMY_FIRE_MAX_DEPTH=0.9 (rules.ts:69) vs the correct 0.857 is a real but smaller gap. Holds in full.

title: Fuseball vulnerability is INVERTED: the arcade's fuseball is killable only while it is rolling between lanes
claim: COLCHK's fuse branch (2965-2979) requires THREE things before a player charge may kill a fuseball: it is not at the rim (INVAY != CURSY, 2969-2970 — a fuseball at the top is entirely bulletproof); the charge's base line matches INVAL1; and INVAL2 is NEGATIVE (bit 7 set). INVAL2 has bit 7 set only while the fuse is mid-jump — JUMPSD writes $81/$87 into it when a lateral jump starts (2025-2034), and JJUMPM writes $20 (positive) into it the instant the fuse lands on a line, with the comment 'MAKE IT INVINCIBLE' (1928). So: rolling between lanes = VULNERABLE, parked on a lane = INVINCIBLE, at the rim = INVINCIBLE. Our model is the exact opposite: `vulnerable` starts false, toggles on each lane slide (fuseball.ts:41), and resolveBulletHits skips the fuseball when it is NOT vulnerable — with a comment stating it is 'killable by a bullet only in its on-lane vulnerable phase... invulnerable while rolling the rim'.
reasoning: This is a correctness inversion, not a tuning error, and it inverts the skill: in the arcade you must time your shot for the moment the fuseball is crossing between lanes; in ours you must wait for it to settle. It also makes our rim fuseball killable where the arcade's cannot be touched at all. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC. COLCHK's fuse branch (2964-2979) requires exactly the three things claimed: INVAY != CURSY (2969-2970, 'FUSE AT TOP?' — an IFNE on the not-equal branch), matching base line (2971-2973), and INVAL2 negative (2974-2976, IFMI before JSR INCFS2, the kill routine). Cross-checked the two writers of INVAL2: JUMPSD (2013-2034) sets it to $81 or $87 (both bit7=1, negative) the instant a lateral jump STARTS; JJUMPM (the jump-processing routine, lines ~1924-1928) sets it to $20 (bit7=0, positive) the instant the fuse LANDS on a line, with the verbatim comment 'MAKE IT INVINCIBLE' at 1928. So mid-jump=negative=vulnerable, landed=positive=invincible — exactly inverted from our code. Read fuseball.ts and sim.ts directly: the code's own comments assert 'killable by a bullet only in its on-lane vulnerable phase... invulnerable while rolling the rim' (fuseball.ts:19-23, state.ts:72-75) and sim.ts:316 skips the bullet hit when NOT vulnerable — the documented and implemented model is the precise opposite of the ROM. Not a unit or macro-decode artifact; a genuine correctness inversion. Holds.

title: Fuseballs do not chase the player before wave 17 — they pick left/right at random
claim: The fuseball's chase behaviour is controlled by WFUSCH (D7 = chase at the top, D6 = chase on the tube, ALCOMN.MAC:786) and comes from TWFUSC (686-690). TWFUSC's FIRST record starts at wave 17, and the table ends in TE — so for every wave below 17, CONTOUR's EOT path (442) yields 0 and WFUSCH = 0, meaning NEITHER chase bit is set. JFUSEUP/MAYBLR (2121-2165) then always take the LEFRIT branch, which chooses the rotation direction from RANDOM (2171-2178). Fuseballs first appear at wave 11 (W-036), so for their entire early-game life they wander. Even from wave 17 the chase is conditional: TR alternates 0/$40 across waves 17-32, and MAYBLR only chases on an EVEN invader index (2157-2159). Our fuseball ALWAYS steps toward the player's lane.
reasoning: Our fuseball is a homing missile from the moment it appears; the arcade's is a drunk that becomes a hunter later. This is the difference between 'fuseballs are the level-11 surprise' and 'fuseballs are unavoidable'. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC and ALCOMN.MAC. WFUSCH is confirmed as 'FUSE CHASE PLAYER FLAG (D7 FOR TOP;D6 FOR TUBE)' (ALCOMN.MAC:786), matching the claim's bit assignments exactly. TWFUSC (686-690) has its first record start at wave 17 and terminates in TE; traced CONTOUR's table-walk (398-469) and confirmed the TE-type-code EOT path (442, 'BEQ TEXIT' with A already 0 from the just-loaded TYPCOD) writes 0 straight into the output byte at TEXIT (461-463) whenever no record's wave range matches — so for any CURWAV<17, WFUSCH=0, both chase bits clear. Verified both consumers respect this: MAYBLR's 'BIT WFUSCH / IFVS' (2155-2156, V flag = bit6/D6) and JFUSEUP's 'LDA WFUSCH / IFMI' (2122-2123, bit7/D7) both fall through to JSR LEFRIT when WFUSCH=0, and LEFRIT (2171-2178) picks direction purely via 'BIT RANDOM / IFVS'. Our rollSpawnKind (rules.ts:253) does gate fuseball introduction at level>=11, consistent with the finding's cross-reference, but stepFuseball (fuseball.ts) takes no level/wave parameter at all and unconditionally computes dir = laneStepToward(tube, e.lane, playerLane) (line 38) — there is no code path by which it could reproduce the wave-17 gate. Holds.

title: On early waves a fuseball turns back before reaching the rim while nymphs remain

source.verbatim: CMP I,20		;YES. TURN BACK BEFORE TOP

ours.verbatim: e.depth = Math.max(0, Math.min(RIM_DEPTH, e.depth + e.direction * speedFor(e, ctx) * ctx.dt))

claim: JFUSEUP (2105-2130): if there are nymphs left AND CURWAV < 17 ('EARLY WAVE?'), the fuseball's climb is capped at Y = $20 (depth 0.929) instead of CURSY — reaching it triggers a lateral move (LEFRIT/FUCHPL) rather than an arrival. Only when the nymph queue is empty does it 'HEAD FOR THE TOP' (2118). It also reverses at the BOTTOM of its range ($80, 2132-2143), so it yo-yos. Our fuseball climbs monotonically to depth 1 and grabs.
reasoning: The arcade's early-game fuseball is a lane-denial hazard that patrols the middle of the well; ours is a killer that always arrives. Note this rule depends on the nymph count (W-002), so it cannot be modelled correctly until nymphs exist — a shared prerequisite with W-029 and W-041. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:2095-2145 (JFUSEUP) line by line, tracking which value is in the accumulator through each conditional (a real risk here since several IF blocks in a row don't reload A). Confirmed the up-climb path: after INVAY is updated (2098-2104) and the arrival clamp against CURSY (2105-2108) is skipped (i.e., not yet arrived), the code falls into 'LDY NYMCOU / IFNE' (nymphs left) / 'LDY CURWAV / CPY 17. / IFCC' (early wave) / 'CMP I,20' (2115) — this CMP reuses the still-live accumulator value (the just-updated INVAY), correctly decoded as the claim states: comparing current climbed position against 0x20. Confirmed via the Y-direction analysis (see W-021) that Y=0x20 sits closer to the rim than Y=CURSY's normal arrival, giving depth (0xf0-0x20)/224 ≈ 0.929 — matching the claim and matching our own SPIKER_TURNAROUND_DEPTH constant's use of the same $20 value for a different enemy. When NYMCOU==0 the code RTS's immediately at 2118 ('NONE LEFT. HEAD FOR TOP'), skipping the cap entirely. Confirmed the yo-yo bottom reversal too: the down-climb branch (2131-2143) calls JSMOVD then 'CMP I,080 / IFCS' (2133-2134, 'AT BOTTOM OF RANGE?'), reversing there. Our fuseball.ts has no nymph count, no wave gate, and monotonically climbs to depth 1 (line 30: 'Math.min(1, e.depth + params.fuseballSpeed * dt)') with no reversal — the divergence is real and the size='m' is fair given it depends on nymphs not yet existing in our sim. Holds.

title: A pulsar flips TOWARD the player; ours flips in a random direction

source.verbatim: PULSC3:	VCHPLA			;SET FLIP DIRECTION TOWARD PLAYER

claim: The pulsar's CAM program is PULSCH (2493-2509): set a loop counter from PUCHDE (the per-wave 'pulsar chase delay', TPUCHDE 680-685), move that many frames, then check whether a pulse is imminent (VCHKPU), then VCHPLA — set the flip direction toward the player via JCHPLA's shortest polar delta — and start the flip. Our stepPulsar draws the direction from the RNG and uses params.flipInterval (rules.ts:185) rather than PUCHDE.
reasoning: The pulsar is supposed to walk deliberately onto your lane and then electrify it; a random walk makes it a slow flipper. PUCHDE is also a real per-wave table (40/20 alternating at waves 17-32, ramping to 20/10 by wave 40+), while our flipInterval is a hand-tuned 1.5/ramp. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC. PULSCH (2493-2509, explicitly commented 'PULSAR CHASER PLAYER') runs VSLOPB PUCHDE (a per-wave delay load), VSPUMO in a loop, VCHKPU (pulse check), then PULSC3: VCHPLA (2504, verbatim as cited) before VJUMPS starts the flip. Decoded the CAM macro dispatch (TABJSR, 1611-1631): VCHPLA maps to JCHPLA (entry 0x24, line 1630), and JCHPLA itself (1876-1889) computes POLDEL between the invader's line and CURSL1, then sets the CW/CCW rotation bit based on the shorter arc ('SEND CHASER SHORTEST WAY') — a deliberate toward-player computation, not randomness. Confirmed TPUCHDE (680-685, PN=40,PC=20) is a real per-wave chase-DELAY table (governs cadence between flips, not direction) — the claim's numeric gloss is a fair simplification of the TZ/TR/TA record mix. Read pulsar.ts directly: stepPulsar's flip branch (line 32) is 'const dir = nextFloat(rng) < 0.5 ? -1 : 1' — pure coin-flip, no player-lane input at all — and uses params.flipInterval (line 34), never PUCHDE. The divergence is real and correctly characterized: the ROM pulsar deliberately homes onto the player's lane before flipping; ours is a random walk. Holds.

title: The pulse is a single GLOBAL phase shared by every pulsar, ~9 frames on / ~31 off (0.32 s of a 1.41 s cycle) — not a per-enemy 0.6 s pulse
claim: MOVINV updates the pulse ONCE per frame, outside the invader loop (1536-1570): PULSON += PULTIM; a sign change of PULSON is the on/off transition (PULSON >= 0 = pulsing); PULSON bounces between +15 and -63 (1558-1568), with PULTIM = 4 for waves 1-48, 6 for 49-64, 8 for 65+ (WPULTIM, 610-613). With PULTIM = 4 the full cycle is ~40 GAME frames, which at the ROM's 28.44 fps (FR-001) is 1.41 s, of which only ~9 frames (~0.32 s) are ON. Every pulsar on the board pulses in unison off this one counter. We give each pulsar its OWN pulseTimer, ON for PULSE_DURATION = 0.6 s and OFF for pulseInterval = max(1.2, 3.0/ramp) s — a 3.6 s cycle at level 1, bottoming out at 1.8 s once the ramp reaches the 1.2 s floor.
reasoning: Our pulse is ~1.9x too long (0.6 s vs 0.32 s), our cycle is ~1.3x too slow even at its floor (1.8 s vs 1.41 s) and 2.6x too slow at level 1, and the phases are independent between pulsars. The arcade's synchronised, short, fast strobe is what makes a pulsar level readable — you learn the beat and move on the gap; independent per-enemy phases make the board unlearnable. Corrected for the 28.444 fps rebase (see FR-001 and FR-012 in pair-11-framerate-adjudication.json): the original magnitudes ('4x too long, 2x too infrequent') converted the ROM's frame counts at 60 Hz — the shape of the divergence is unchanged, but it is roughly half the size that was filed. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALCOMN.MAC:774-775: PULSON and PULTIM are each `.BLKB 1` — single global bytes, not per-invader fields. Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:1508-1571 (MOVINV): the invader loop runs and exits (DEC INDEX1/MIEND, line 1533-1535) BEFORE the pulse-update block at 1536-1571, so the pulse math genuinely executes once per frame, outside/after the per-enemy loop, as claimed. Decoded the update: LDA PULSON/ADC PULTIM/TAY/EOR PULSON/STY PULSON (1537-1542) computes new=old+PULTIM and detects a sign flip via XOR — turn ON if the new value is non-negative, OFF if negative, exactly as described. WPULTIM (610-613) confirmed byte-for-byte: 4 for waves 1-48, 6 for 49-64, 8 for 65+. Checked the bounce thresholds by decoding 1557-1570 myself rather than trusting the (stale/typo'd) comment 'BOUNCE BETWEEN -27 AND +15' at 1558 — the actual immediates compared are CMP I,15. and CMP I,-63. (1559, 1562), so the finding's +15/-63 bounce is right and it correctly ignored the wrong comment. I simulated the exact byte machine in Python to check the derived timing: with PULTIM=4 the steady-state cycle is 42 frames (not ~40), of which 9 are ON — so the 9-frame ON claim is exact, but the 40-frame total is undercounted by ~5%. Our side (pulsar.ts:22-27) gives each Pulsar its own independent pulseTimer/pulsing pair, seeded per-enemy in sim.ts:117 — there is no shared global phase anywhere in the codebase (grepped for a single shared pulse clock; none exists). This is a real, large, correctly-identified structural divergence. [CORRECTION] The full pulse cycle is ~42 ROM frames, not ~40 — 42/28.444 = 1.48 s, not 1.41 s (verified by simulating the exact PULSON/PULTIM byte machine). The 9-frame / 0.32 s ON duration is exact. Everything else (global vs. per-enemy phase, the structural description of the update) is accurate.

title: A pulse only kills when the pulsar is above the potency height (PULPOT)

ours.verbatim: const killer = grabber ?? s.enemies.find((e) => e.kind === 'pulsar' && e.pulsing && e.lane === pl)

claim: JPULMO's kill test (1802-1817) requires PULSON to be positive (pulse on) AND INVAY < PULPOT AND both cursor legs to match the pulsar's legs. PULPOT is $A0 for waves 1-64 and $C0 for 65+ (WPULPOT, 606-609) — $A0 is depth 0.357 in our convention. So a pulsing pulsar deeper than 0.357 (i.e. in the far third of the well) does NOT kill. Our resolvePlayerHits kills whenever a pulsing pulsar shares the player's lane, at ANY depth, including one that has just hatched at the far end.
reasoning: One extra depth condition. Without it, the player's lane becomes instantly lethal the moment a pulsar spawns anywhere on it, which is not a thing the arcade does. (We already have the correct PULPOT-derived constant in rules.ts:125 — PULSAR_NEAR_FAR_DEPTH — for the SPEED test; the kill test needs the same threshold.) [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:1802-1817 (tail of JPULMO). Decoded in full: `LDA PULSON; IFPL` gates on pulse-on (matches PULSON's positive=on convention already established for W-026). `LDA X,INVAY; CMP PULPOT; IFCC` — IFCC is carry-clear, i.e. INVAY < PULPOT (unsigned) — only then do the two lane-match checks and JSR INPPSQ (kill) follow. Since along decreases as depth increases (confirmed via JSMOVU/JSMOVD at 1740-1776, and independently by the existing PULSAR_NEAR_FAR_DEPTH/SPIKER_TURNAROUND_DEPTH derivations in rules.ts which use the same (0xf0-along)/224 mapping), INVAY < PULPOT($A0) means depth > (0xf0-0xa0)/224 ≈ 0.357 — i.e. the kill test requires the pulsar to have already crossed INTO the potency zone (nearer 2/3 of the well). A pulsar still in the far third (depth <= 0.357, e.g. freshly hatched) cannot kill even while pulsing. Re-read our sim.ts:394 exactly as cited: `s.enemies.find((e) => e.kind === 'pulsar' && e.pulsing && e.lane === pl)` — no depth condition of any kind. The claim is accurate and the fix is trivial (add `&& e.depth >= PULSAR_NEAR_FAR_DEPTH`, reusing the existing constant as the finding notes).

title: Pulsar dual climb speed: the STRUCTURE (flipper speed below the potency height, spd_pulsar above) is right, but BOTH speeds are 2.11x too fast
claim: JPULMO (1780-1788) selects the speed index: ZABPUL by default, but if the pulsar is going up and INVAY >= PULPOT (i.e. it is still deeper than the potency height) it swaps to ZABFLI — 'NO. GO FASTER'. WINVIL+ZABPUL / WINVIN+ZABPUL are hard-set to $A0/$FE (547-550) = -1.375 along-units per GAME frame, level-independent — the same byte as the wave-1 flipper. At the ROM's real 28.44 fps (FR-001) that is 1.375 * (256/9) / 224 = 0.175 depth/s. We ship PULSAR_CLIMB_SPEED = 82.5/224 = 0.368 depth/s, and 82.5 is literally 1.375 * 60: the constant carries the invented frame rate on its face. PULSAR_NEAR_FAR_DEPTH = (0xf0-0xa0)/224 = 0.357 is a pure position ratio and IS correct, and the finding's structural half (dual speed, selected exactly this way) stands. The pulsar's other speed — params.flipperSpeed — is 2.11x fast for the same reason (FR-008).
reasoning: Originally CONFIRMED on the grounds that 'both the threshold and the two speeds are right'. The threshold is right; the two speeds are both 2.11x the arcade's. Corrected for the 28.444 fps rebase (see FR-009 in pair-11-framerate-adjudication.json): reclassed CONFIRMED → DIVERGENCE, keeping the structural half intact — PULSAR_CLIMB_SPEED must become 1.375 * ROM_FPS / 224 = 0.175 depth/s, and because it IS the L1 flipper byte it has to be rebased in the same edit as the flipper table (FR-008) or the two desynchronise. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:1780-1788 (JPULMO climb-up branch). Decoded fully: LDY I,ZABPUL sets the default speed slot; IFPL (going up) checks CMP PULPOT/IFCS — carry SET means INVAY >= PULPOT($A0), i.e. still in the far portion (depth <= 0.357) — and only then swaps LDY to ZABFLI ('NO. GO FASTER'), otherwise keeping ZABPUL. This is: far portion -> flipper speed (level-dependent, faster at high levels), near portion -> the fixed ZABPUL constant. Cross-checked against our pulsar.ts:19 (`e.depth >= PULSAR_NEAR_FAR_DEPTH ? PULSAR_CLIMB_SPEED : params.flipperSpeed`): near (depth>=0.357) -> fixed PULSAR_CLIMB_SPEED, far (depth<0.357) -> flipperSpeed. Structurally identical mapping — confirmed correct. Verified the magnitude claim: WINVIL+ZABPUL/WINVIN+ZABPUL are hardcoded at ALWELG.MAC:547-550 to $A0/$FE every wave (inside CONTOUR, not a per-level WTABLE entry) = -1.375 along/frame in 8.8 fixed point (-2 + 160/256), level-independent, matching the L1 flipper byte (FLIPPER_ALONG_PER_FRAME_L1=1.375 in rules.ts:148). PULSAR_CLIMB_SPEED = 82.5/224 in rules.ts:120, and 82.5 = 1.375*60 — the 60 Hz assumption is baked into the numerator exactly as claimed. At the real 28.444 fps that constant should be 1.375*28.444/224 ≈ 0.175 depth/s versus our shipped 0.368 depth/s: ratio 2.104, matching the audit's already-established 2.11x-too-fast correction factor (60/28.444=2.109). The reclassification from 'both speeds correct' to 'structure correct, both speeds 2.11x fast' is accurate and consistent with the ground-truth framerate rebase.

title: Pulsars descend as well as climb — they yo-yo while nymphs remain
claim: The pulsar's INVAC2 carries a direction bit (INVDIR, ALCOMN.MAC:869). JPULMO (1789-1801) moves it DOWN when the bit is set, and reverses it back to UP once it descends past PULPOT — unless the nymph queue is empty, in which case it forces the pulsar up ('SEND PULSAR UP', 1793). CHASER (1829-1837) likewise sends a pulsar that reaches the rim back DOWN if any nymphs remain. So a pulsar patrols up and down between the rim and the potency height for as long as the wave still has nymphs. Our pulsar's depth only ever increases.
reasoning: Same prerequisite as W-024: the reversal is keyed on NYMCOU, so this cannot be modelled correctly until nymphs exist (W-002). The visible effect is large — arcade pulsars hover and strobe in the middle of the well; ours march to the rim and park. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:1789-1801 (JPULMO down-branch) and 1821-1849 (CHASER). Down-branch: JSR JSMOVD moves the pulsar toward the far end (verified JSMOVD at 1764-1776 subtracts a negative speed, i.e. increases INVAY = moves away from rim); then LDY NYMCOU/IFEQ forces A=0xFF ('SEND PULSAR UP' comment at 1793) only when the nymph count is zero — otherwise A keeps the real INVAY from JSMOVD's return. Either way, CMP PULPOT/IFCS then flips INVAC2's direction bit (EOR INVDIR) — reversing to 'up' once genuinely past PULPOT (depth back to <=0.357), or unconditionally when nymphs are gone. CHASER (1829-1837): when a pulsar (CMP ZABPUL/IFEQ) reaches the rim and NYMCOU != 0, it flips INVAC2 (EOR INVDIR) and RETURNS EARLY, skipping the normal become-a-chaser path entirely — so as long as nymphs remain, a pulsar can never grab/chase, it just bounces back down. Cross-checked ALCOMN.MAC:869 (INVDIR=$80, 0=up/1=down) and :916 (NYMCOU, single counter) — both real, both as cited. Grepped our codebase for 'nymph' (case-insensitive): zero hits anywhere in src — nymphs are wholly unmodeled, exactly as the finding's stated prerequisite (W-002) says. Re-read pulsar.ts:20 as cited: `e.depth = Math.min(1, e.depth + climbSpeed * dt)` is a one-directional clamp-to-1 accumulation; there is no code path anywhere in stepPulsar that decreases depth. The yo-yo-while-nymphs-remain behavior is real, our monotonic-climb behavior is real, and the finding is honest about depending on the (also-missing) nymph mechanic.

title: A tanker's children are born at the PARENT's exact depth — above the grab line — not clamped to SPLIT_CHILD_DEPTH = 0.85
claim: KILINV (2300-2302) opens by saving the DYING PARENT's own along value — `LDA Y,INVAY / STA TEMP0` — and ACTINV (1219-1226), called once per child off KILINV's tail, seats each one straight back out of it: `LDA TEMP0 / STA Y,INVAY` (line 1226, cited). Both children are therefore born at the parent's EXACT depth; there is no clamp anywhere in the cabinet. Ours clamped them with `Math.min(t.depth, SPLIT_CHILD_DEPTH)` where SPLIT_CHILD_DEPTH = 0.85 — a deliberate softening whose own comment said so ('Must be < PLAYER_RIM_DEPTH (0.92) so a rim-split is not an instant grab') and which predates this epic. It matters because a carrier that arrives under its own steam bursts at $20 = depth 0.9286, so the children are born high in the well rather than at a soft 0.85. [CORRECTED, tp1-27] This finding originally added that they are therefore 'born lethal' and grab a flanking player on the burst frame. That was wrong, and it was wrong because it compared the child's depth against our INVENTED PLAYER_RIM_DEPTH = 0.92. The real grab line is the rim (CURSY = $10 = depth 1.0) and the grab is reachable only from the CHASER cam (W-049), and ATOP is tested BEFORE the carrier check (1744-1750) — so a carrier that reaches the rim becomes a chaser instead of bursting, and a newborn child is ALWAYS below the grab line. No child is ever born lethal; it must climb to the rim first.
reasoning:
[SCOPE, tp1-24] This finding originally asserted TWO divergences: (1) the carrier bursts at $20 (depth 0.9286), not our invented TANKER_SPLIT_DEPTH = 0.9, and (2) the children are born at the parent's depth, not clamped to SPLIT_CHILD_DEPTH = 0.85. Divergence (1) was closed by tp1-5, which made TANKER_SPLIT_DEPTH an alias of SPLIT_TOO_CLOSE_DEPTH so that the arrival gate and SPLCHA's too-close test read the one ROM byte (they had been two numbers, which is what made W-032's no-flip branch dead code for a whole review cycle). tp1-5 deliberately did NOT stamp a remediated_by, because half the finding was still open and the stamp would have been a lie. The title, claim and source anchor above have been rescoped to divergence (2) alone — the half that was still live — and it is that half `remediated_by` names. The original ROM-side verification, which is the durable authority here and is unchanged by any of this, follows.

[REFUTATION, original] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:1740-1762 (JSMOVU tail). Decoded the branch structure: 1744-1747 handles reaching the rim (CMP CURSY/BEQ ATOP, or IFCC->ATOP) via CHASER; the ELSE at 1748 is the not-yet-at-rim path, where CMP I,20/IFCC (line 1750) checks INVAY < $20 (hex, no trailing period, consistent with this file's convention — confirmed against the identical $20 already used for SPIKER_TURNAROUND_DEPTH) using the ALREADY-UPDATED INVAY from lines 1741-1743. Only then, if the invader is actually carrying cargo (AND INVCAR), does it call KILINV. INVAY < $20 => depth = (0xf0-along)/224 > (0xf0-0x20)/224 ≈ 0.929. Re-opened KILINV (2300-2302): `LDA Y,INVAY; STA TEMP0` saves the PARENT's own along value. Followed TEMP0 through to ACTINV (1219-1226): `LDA TEMP0; STA Y,INVAY` — the new invader's INVAY is set directly from the parent's saved depth, for both children (ACTINV is called twice at KILINV's tail, once per child, both reading the same TEMP0). Both halves of the claim verified byte-accurate.

[REMEDIATION, tp1-24] The clamp is deleted — splitTanker now seats both children at `t.depth`, and SPLIT_CHILD_DEPTH is removed from rules.ts rather than renumbered, because the children's depth is not a constant at all: it is the parent's. This was ratified as a deliberate DIFFICULTY CHANGE, not a transcription fix. Measured on wave 3 (children on the flanking lanes 4 and 6 of a tanker bursting on lane 5): a player standing on a landing lane now dies on the burst frame, where before he had ~22 frames. It is shippable because the other two thirds of the same ROM mechanism carry the fairness — splitTanker VACATES the parent's own lane (so the player who tracked the tanker and stood on its lane to shoot it is the one player it cannot grab), and the no-flip rule (W-032, NEWGEN) means the children cannot flip ONTO him. Measured, that no-flip rule doubles his reaction window on the vacated lane: he survives 18 frames with it, 9 without. Burst depth, child depth and no-flip are ONE mechanism; tp1-24 landed the last third.

[CORRECTION, tp1-27] The REMEDIATION note above states that after tp1-24 'a player standing on a landing lane now dies on the burst frame', and ratified that as the authentic difficulty. It is not authentic — it is an artefact of the invented grab line this finding was measured against (see W-049). With PLAYER_RIM_DEPTH derived from CURSY ($10 = depth 1.0), the grab line sits ABOVE the burst line ($20 = 0.9286), so a child born at the parent's depth is born BELOW it and cannot grab until it has climbed to the rim and become a chaser. The player on a landing lane gets his reaction window back. What tp1-24 actually landed — and what stands — is that the children are born at the parent's EXACT depth with no clamp; that half is byte-verified above and is unaffected.

title: Split children straddle the parent — lanes L-1 and L+1, parent's lane left empty
claim: KILINV computes the first child's line as INVAL1 - 1 (2331-2333) and the second's as that value + 2 (2352-2355), both masked to 0F — so the two children land on the lanes either side of the dead carrier and nothing is left on its own lane. Our splitTanker emits children on wrapLane(lane-1) and wrapLane(lane+1).
reasoning: Exact match, including the wrap. (The arcade additionally ORs ZROCCW into the second child's status, 2364-2366, so the pair flips in OPPOSITE directions — that part we cannot express until W-007.)

title: Children of a split that happens near the rim are given the NON-FLIPPING cam
claim: SPLCHA (1494-1505) compares the split depth (TEMP0) against $20 and, if the split is happening within $20 of the rim, routes the child through NEWGEN instead of NEWTY2. NEWGEN assigns the type's DEFAULT cam from TNEWCAM (1483-1484), which for a flipper is NOJUMP — 'YES. NO FLIPPING'. NEWTY2, the normal path, would have given the flipper the wave's WFLICAM program. So a tanker that auto-splits at the rim (the common case — see W-030) always produces two flippers that can only climb, never flip. We have no cam and no such rule; our split children are ordinary enemies.
reasoning: This is the fairness valve that makes the rim-split survivable: two flippers appearing on the lanes either side of you at depth 0.929 would be an unavoidable death if they could immediately flip onto you. Its absence in our build is masked only because our children spawn lower (0.85) and our grab line is 0.92. Fixing W-030 without also fixing this would make rim splits lethal.

title: Tankers carry ONLY flippers until wave 33; fuseball cargo starts at 33 and pulsar cargo at 41
claim: NEWTAN (1445-1474) picks the cargo by drawing a random index 0-3 into WTACAR and walking it until it finds a type with openings. WTACAR has four entries: slots 0 and 1 are hard-set to ZCARFL on every wave (CONTOUR, 551-553); slot 2 comes from WWTAC2 (614-617) = ZCARFL for waves 1-32, ZCARFU for 33-40, ZCARPU for 41-99; slot 3 comes from WWTAC3 (618-620) = ZCARFL for waves 1-48, ZCARFU for 49-99. So ALL FOUR slots are flippers for waves 1-32: a tanker cannot carry anything but flippers until wave 33, cannot carry a pulsar until wave 41, and the pulsar cargo lives in only one of four slots even then. We roll fuseball cargo from level 11 and pulsar cargo from level 17, at weights 4 against flipper's 10.
reasoning: Our tankers manufacture fuseballs 22 levels early and pulsars 24 levels early. Combined with W-035 (tankers appearing at wave 3) this materially changes the enemy mix of the entire first third of the game. The fix is to replace rollTankerCargo's gates with the WTACAR table. [REFUTATION] Re-opened NEWTAN (ALWELG.MAC:1445-1474): draws RANDOM AND 3 into Y, walks WTACAR[Y] (LDX Y,WTACAR at 1463) looking for an open slot, confirming WTACAR is indeed a 4-slot table read by index. Confirmed WTACAR is `.BLKB 4` (ALCOMN.MAC:776) and ZCARNO/ZCARFL/ZCARPU/ZCARFU = 0/1/2/3 (ALCOMN.MAC:861-864). Traced how each of the 4 slots is populated: CONTOUR itself (ALWELG.MAC:551-553) hardcodes WTACAR+0 and WTACAR+1 to ZCARFL on every single call — these two slots are NOT in the per-level WTABLE dispatch list at all (checked WTABLE, lines 728-751ish; only WTACAR+2 and WTACAR+3 appear, at 745-746, pointing at WWTAC2/WWTAC3). WWTAC2 (614-617) and WWTAC3 (618-620) transcribed and match the finding exactly: slot 2 = ZCARFL(1-32)/ZCARFU(33-40)/ZCARPU(41-99); slot 3 = ZCARFL(1-48)/ZCARFU(49-99). So for waves 1-32 all four slots are literally ZCARFL — a tanker can only ever produce flipper cargo — fuseball cargo first becomes possible at wave 33 (slot 2), and pulsar cargo first becomes possible at wave 41 (slot 2 only; slot 3 never reaches ZCARPU in this table). Cross-checked our rollTankerCargo (rules.ts:263-270) exactly as cited: fuseball gated at level>=11, pulsar at level>=17, both weight 4 vs flipper's 10, with no wave-dependent slot structure at all. The claim's numbers (11 vs 33, 17 vs 41) and mechanism (WTACAR 4-slot table vs our flat weighted table) are both verified correct.

title: Enemy type selection is a per-type MIN/MAX population solver, not a weighted random table

ours.verbatim: export function rollSpawnKind(level: number, rng: Rng): EnemyKind {

claim: NYMCHA (1266-1412) decides what a hatching nymph becomes, in this order: (1) compute per-type 'openings' = WFLMAX[type] - FLIPCO[type] (the live count of that type) for all 5 types; (2) subtract 2 openings of the carried type for EVERY live tanker, so cargo is reserved in advance (1286-1303); (3) clamp openings to the total free invader slots; (4) if exactly one type has openings, take it; (5) otherwise satisfy any type whose live count is below its WFLMIN first (1351-1364); (6) then attempt a 'smart launch' — if both a spiker and a tanker slot are free, look at the enemy line the nymph is standing on and launch a SPIKER on a short/dead line or a TANKER on a long one (1366-1385); (7) failing all that, draw RANDO2 AND 3, add 1 — a random type that EXCLUDES flippers (1386-1389) — and walk from there to the first needy type. Our rollSpawnKind is a single weighted pick from a fixed table (flipper 10, tanker 4, spiker 3, pulsar 3, fuseball 3) with level gates and a per-cycle 'hard' multiplier.
reasoning: The arcade's board composition is a CONSTRAINT SATISFACTION over the live population — it guarantees the wave's minimums, never exceeds its maximums, reserves slots for the contents of tankers already on the board, and biases spikers toward lanes that already have short spikes. Ours is memoryless: it can roll five flippers in a row on a wave whose maximum is 4, and it will never guarantee the wave's required spiker. This is the second-largest rewrite in the audit after the CAM, and it depends on nymphs (W-002) and the slot cap (W-004). [REFUTATION] Re-opened NYMCHA (ALWELG.MAC:1266-1412) line-by-line; every sub-range the finding cites checks out exactly: 1275-1282 is the per-type WFLMAX-FLIPCO max-check (LDA X,WFLMAX / SEC / SBC X,FLIPCO / IFCS / STA X,OPFLIP); 1286-1303 is the tanker-cargo-reservation loop (LDY WINVMX...DEC X,OPFLIP-1 twice per live carrier); 1351-1364 is the WFLMIN starvation check; 1366-1385 is the smart-launch block (OPSPIN/OPTANK nonzero -> read LINEY at the nymph's own lane via TEMP1, CMP against $CC to pick spiker on a short/dead line vs tanker on a long one); 1386-1389 is RANDO2 AND 3 (+1), which excludes type 0 (flipper) exactly as claimed. Verified CONTOUR (ALWELG.MAC:397-440) confirms every WTABLE-driven array (WFLMAX/WFLMIN/WTANMX.../TELIHI) is indexed by CURWAV, the live wave counter -- so 'per-type population solver' is the right frame, not a misread. Checked our side: rollSpawnKind (rules.ts:244-256) is a single stateless weightedPick with no per-type cap; grepped sim.ts and found no other code anywhere that enforces a max, a min guarantee, or reads live enemy counts by kind before spawning (src/core/sim.ts:130 calls rollSpawnKind directly against a flat budget counter, nothing else touches WFLMAX-equivalent logic). The 'memoryless' characterization holds completely. No unit, radix, or dead-module issues -- ALWELG.MAC shipped, no SPACG-gated block involved. This is the single most airtight finding in the batch.

title: Tankers first appear on wave 3 and spikers on wave 4 — not both on level 5
claim: WTANMX (651-657) itemises the tanker MAX for waves 1-5 as 0, 0, 1, 0, 1 — so exactly one tanker is allowed on wave 3, none on wave 4, one on wave 5, then 2 for waves 6-16. WTANMI (645-650) itemises the MIN as 0, 0, 1, 0 — wave 3 REQUIRES its tanker. WSPIMX (628-635) itemises the spiker max for waves 1-6 as 0, 0, 0, 2, 3, 4 — the first spiker is possible on wave 4 — and WSPIMI (621-627) makes wave 4 require one. Our gates are `level >= 5` for both, with weights rather than counts.
reasoning: Off by two waves for the tanker and one for the spiker, and the non-monotonic zero at wave 4 (tankers vanish again) is not expressible in our weight model at all. Note the tables also blank out later: WSPIMX has no record for waves 17-19, 33-34 or 40-42, so spikers disappear entirely on those waves. [REFUTATION] Directly inspected the raw .BYTE data at the cited lines: WSPIMI:621 = TZ,1,4,0,0,0,1 (waves1-4 min = 0,0,0,1); WSPIMX:628 = TZ,1,6,0,0,0,2,3,4 (waves1-6 max = 0,0,0,2,3,4); WTANMI:645 = TZ,1,4,0,0,1,0; WTANMX:651 = TZ,1,5,0,0,1,0,1 -- byte-for-byte exactly what the finding states. Decoded the TZ/T1/TE/TZANDF/TA/TB/TR type codes from their real definitions at ALWELG.MAC:408-414 (not guessed) to confirm TZ means 'one byte per wave in the declared range' -- so these are literal per-wave min/max counts, not some other unit. Confirmed via CONTOUR (:397-440, LDA CURWAV -> STA TEMP2) that these tables index on the actual game wave/level counter, and confirmed our level counter starts at 1 with no off-by-one (state.ts:138, level:1) so the wave-vs-level comparison is apples-to-apples. The claimed gaps in WSPIMX (17-19, 33-34, 40-42 falling through to 0) are real -- verified no .BYTE line covers those ranges. rules.ts:250 verbatim matches ('tanker', level >= 5 ? ...). One thing worth flagging in corrections: our own code carries a prior, documented 'stakeholder decision' (rules.ts:233-241, citing docs/ux/2026-06-27-enemy-roster-rom-extract.md) asserting tankers/spikers both start at L5+ per an earlier ROM read. That earlier internal doc and this finding's direct read of ALWELG.MAC disagree; since the raw .BYTE data is unambiguous and the primary source per this audit's rules, the finding's numbers stand -- the earlier internal doc looks to be the one that was wrong (or read a different table/revision). [CORRECTION] Flag for the user: this directly contradicts an existing internal doc (docs/ux/2026-06-27-enemy-roster-rom-extract.md) that the codebase already cites as its ROM source for the L5+ gates. That doc should be re-checked, not just rules.ts.

title: Fuseballs first appear on wave 11 and pulsars on wave 17
claim: WPULMX's first record starts at wave 17 (663) and WPULMI's at wave 17 (659); with no earlier record the CONTOUR scan falls through to TE and yields 0, so the pulsar max is 0 below wave 17. WFUSMX's first record is T1,11.,16.,1 (672) and WFUSMI's is the same (667), so the fuseball max is 0 below wave 11. Our gates are `level >= 17` for pulsars and `level >= 11` for fuseballs (rules.ts:252-253).
reasoning: Both introduction waves are exactly right. (The tables also have GAPS we do not model — no fuseballs on waves 17-21 or 26, and the pulsar max is 5 on wave 17 alone before settling at 2-3 — but the introduction wave itself is correct.)

title: From wave 4 every lane STARTS with a spike already on it
claim: TELIHI is a TZANDF table (indexed by (wave-1) mod 16) giving NWTELI, the INITIAL height of every enemy line for the new wave. INIENE (303-313) writes that one value into LINEY for all NLINES=16 lines. The bytes are 0, 0, 0, $E0, $D8, $D4, $D0, $C8, $C0, $B8, $B0, $A8, $A0, $A0, $A0, $A8 — so waves 1-3 start clean, and from wave 4 EVERY lane begins with a spike, growing from depth ($F0-$E0)/224 = 0.071 at wave 4 to ($F0-$A0)/224 = 0.357 by wave 13. We reset every lane to zero on every level (advanceLevel, sim.ts:558) and grow spikes only from spikers.
reasoning: This is a whole missing layer of the level design: from wave 4 onward the well is pre-seeded with a floor of spikes that the player must shoot down before the warp, and every wave's spiker work is ADDITIVE on top of it. Our waves 4-16 are visibly emptier than the arcade's, and our warp is correspondingly safer. [REFUTATION] TELIHI at ALWELG.MAC:696 matches verbatim. Confirmed .WORD TELIHI,NWTELI (line 749) and NWTELI's use in INIENE (LDA NWTELI at line 305, then BEGIN/STA X,LINEY/DEX/MIEND writing it into every LINEY[X] for X=NLINES-1 downto 0) -- so one table value really is stamped across every lane at wave start, exactly as claimed. NLINES=16 confirmed in the shipped ALCOMN.MAC:810 (not the unshipped ALVROM values of 15/11, which are unrelated vertex counts, not lane counts). Decoded TZANDF's handler (DOTZAN, ALWELG.MAC:791-799): it computes reduced_wave = ((wave-1) AND 0xF)+1 before falling into the itemized-byte lookup, which resolves to array index (reduced_wave - 1); for wave 4 this lands on index 3 = $E0, for wave 13 on index 12 = $A0 -- reproducing the finding's own worked numbers exactly. Confirmed the depth-conversion constants used (ILINDDY=$F0, ILINLIY=$10, both at ALCOMN.MAC:819-820, so divisor 224 = $F0-$10) are real named ROM constants, not an invented unit -- and they independently match our own code's WARP_ALONG_SPAN=0xf0-0x10=224 (rules.ts:44). Our side (sim.ts:558, unconditional fill(0) on every level) is correctly cited as the reset that erases this layer entirely. Solid. [CORRECTION] Note the array padding: TELIHI actually carries 20 raw bytes after its start/end header, but the AND-0xF indexing only ever reaches the first 16 (indices 0-15); the trailing 4 bytes (0A0,9C,9A,98) are dead data. Doesn't change the finding's conclusion.

title: The spiker turns around at Y = $20 (depth 0.929)
claim: JSTRAI (2222-2229) compares the spiker's INVAY against $20; when it climbs past it ('MAX HEIGHT?'), it sets the ZDIRDO bit to send it back down and pins INVAY at $20. That is depth ($F0-$20)/224 = 0.929, which is exactly our SPIKER_TURNAROUND_DEPTH (rules.ts:115).
reasoning: Correct, including the choice to keep it separate from the spike-height cap. (The spike-height cap itself is wrong — see W-039.)

title: Spikes can reach depth 0.929, not 0.75
claim: JSTRAI (2214-2221) writes the spiker's own INVAY straight into LINEY whenever it is higher than the current line height — the spike IS the spiker's high-water mark, with no cap other than the spiker's own $20 turnaround (W-038). So a fully grown spike reaches depth 0.929, essentially the rim. We clamp the stored spike height to SPIKE_MAX_DEPTH = 0.75 (rules.ts:28) even though we already turn the spiker around at 0.929 — so the last 0.18 of the spiker's climb lays no spike at all.
reasoning: Spike height is the entire warp-crash threat model. Our tallest possible spike stops well short of the rim, so an unattended spiker is far less punishing than the arcade's — and W-037's pre-seeded spikes plus a 0.929 ceiling is what makes late-level warps genuinely dangerous. The cap and the turnaround should be the same $20. [REFUTATION] JSTRAI citation is exact: line 2216 verbatim is 'IFCC ;NEW ENEMY LINE?', and the 2214-2221 range covers LDA X,INVAY / CMP Y,LINEY / IFCC / STA Y,LINEY / LDA I,80 / STA Y,LINSTA / comment / ENDIF -- confirmed the LINEY-update block runs on the RAW (pre-clamp) INVAY value, and the INVAY-clamp-to-$20-and-reverse logic runs immediately AFTER in the same routine (not before), so the high-water mark genuinely can reach the $20 turnaround before the position itself is clamped. Independently re-derived the finding's own arithmetic from real named constants (ILINDDY=$F0, ILINLIY=$10 -> divisor 224) rather than trusting its output: (0xF0-0x20)/224 = 0.929, matching. Checked our code directly (spiker.ts:12-15): stepSpiker turns around at SPIKER_TURNAROUND_DEPTH (~0.929, rules.ts:115), while sim.ts:176 clamps the stored spike to SPIKE_MAX_DEPTH (0.75, rules.ts:28) -- both citations verbatim-correct, confirming the gap is real in our own code today. [CORRECTION] This is not an overlooked bug -- rules.ts:110-115 already documents the exact same 0.929 figure and explicitly states SPIKER_TURNAROUND_DEPTH is kept SEPARATE from SPIKE_MAX_DEPTH on purpose ('story 6-15 deviations') so raising the turnaround doesn't also grow spikes, for warp-balance reasons. The technical divergence is accurate as stated, but recommendation='fix' should account for the fact this was a conscious, already-tracked design decision, not something nobody noticed.

title: A spiker that bottoms out hops to the NEEDIEST lane (shortest/empty spike); we send it to the TALLEST

ours.verbatim: if (s.spikes[i] > tallest) { tallest = s.spikes[i]; target = i }

claim: ASTRAL (2252-2291) is the spiker's relocation routine. Starting from a RANDOM line, it walks all 16 lines and keeps the one with the LARGEST LINEY — and a dead line (LINEY = 0) is scored as $FF, the 'worst case' (2267-2270). Because LINEY is a depth-from-the-rim measure where a larger value means a LOWER tip, the largest LINEY is the SHORTEST spike, and an empty lane beats every spike. So the spiker deliberately goes to the lane that needs a spike most. Our spiker hop (sim.ts:166-175) scans for the TALLEST standing spike and goes there, falling back to a random lane only when no spike exists at all.
reasoning: The polarity is inverted. The arcade's spiker spreads its spikes across the well (which is why late levels end up with a spike on nearly every lane); ours piles them all onto one lane, leaving the rest of the well clean and the warp trivially dodgeable. One comparison operator, but it changes the shape of every board. [REFUTATION] ASTRAL (ALWELG.MAC:2252-2291) confirmed: cited line 2272 verbatim 'IFCS ;NEEDIEST LINE SO FAR?' is exact. Traced the polarity precisely: TEMP4 starts at 0 (running max), loop reads LINEY[Y] (or forces $FF if LINEY==0, i.e. a dead/vacant line), CMP TEMP4, IFCS (A>=TEMP4) updates TEMP4:=A and TEMP0:=Y -- so the routine keeps walking toward and settling on the line with the numerically LARGEST LINEY. Independently confirmed (via JSTRAI, same file) that LINEY is inversely related to spike height: it's set from INVAY, and lower INVAY = closer to rim = taller spike, so a small LINEY = tall spike and a large LINEY (up to the explicit $FF dead-line sentinel) = short or absent spike. Therefore ASTRAL provably biases toward the shortest/emptiest lane, exactly as claimed, and a dead lane always wins over any live spike. Also confirmed ASTRAL is invoked specifically when the spiker bottoms out at the far end (CMP I,0F2 / IFCS 'MIN HEIGHT?' immediately preceding the JSR ASTRAL in JSTRAI), matching the title's 'bottoms out' framing. Our sim.ts:169 verbatim matches ('if (s.spikes[i] > tallest)...') -- confirmed opposite polarity, plus the random-fallback-only-when-zero-spikes-exist behavior (sim.ts:171-173) which is also a narrower trigger than the ROM's true random-walk-every-time. Holds cleanly.

title: A bottomed-out spiker with no nymphs left converts into a flipper-carrying tanker
claim: JSTRAI (2236-2247): when the spiker reaches the bottom of its range and NYMCOU is zero, it rewrites INVAC2's carrier bits to ZCARFL and INVAC1's appearance bits to ZABTAN — it becomes a tanker carrying flippers, and CAMSTA is set to 0 so the CAM branches it out of TRALUP into NOJUMP (2381). Our spiker does exactly this when s.spawn.remaining === 0, spawning a flipper-carrying tanker at depth 0.
reasoning: Correct in mechanism and in cargo, and our spawn.remaining is a faithful stand-in for NYMCOU here. Recorded because it is a non-obvious rule we got right.

title: The Superzapper kills tankers (it strips their cargo first); ours spares them entirely

source.verbatim: LDA Y,INVAC2		;MAKE SURE IT'S NOT A CARRIER

claim: KILENE's EXIKIL (3562-3566) takes the first live invader it finds, CLEARS the INVCAR bits in its INVAC2, and then explodes it — the carrier bit is stripped precisely so that the kill does not split it. So the arcade's zap DOES destroy tankers; it just denies you the cargo. Our first-zap cadence and our kill-count both filter tankers out (`e.kind !== 'tanker'`, sim.ts:476 and 513), so a tanker survives a full superzap.
reasoning: Ours can leave the board un-clearable by a zap that the arcade would clear. The 'declaw' intent (a zap must not manufacture enemies) is right, but the arcade achieves it by stripping the cargo, not by skipping the target. [REFUTATION] KILENE/EXIKIL (ALWELG.MAC:3542-3567) confirmed verbatim: EXIKIL unconditionally clears the INVCAR bits (AND I,^C<INVCAR>, INVCAR=3 per ALCOMN.MAC:860) on whatever invader KILENE's loop found first-alive (no type filter anywhere in the loop at 3549-3556), then JMP INCISQ starts the explosion -- so the ROM absolutely can and does kill tankers via superzap, it just strips carrier status first so no split occurs. This is the correct, precise reading. Checked our code directly: sim.ts:476 (runZapFrame, the sustained first-press cadence) and sim.ts:513 (killCount event) both filter (e) => e.kind !== 'tanker', confirmed verbatim. [CORRECTION] The claim 'ours spares them entirely' overstates scope. That's true only of the FIRST-press sustained wipe cadence (sim.ts:476/513), which our own code comments (sim.ts:485-487) already document as a deliberate choice ('Spares tankers... vaporises one non-tanker per active frame'). The SECOND press (stepZap's used-once->spent branch) calls nearestRimIndex(s, () => true) with no kind filter and zapKillAt never releases cargo -- so that shot already kills tankers without splitting them, matching the ROM's carrier-strip behavior. The real, narrower divergence is: the sustained multi-frame wipe should also be hitting tankers (cargo-stripped) instead of skipping them outright; the single-shot zap is already correct.

title: The first zap window is 19 frames and kills once every OTHER frame (8 kills max); ours is 13 frames, one kill per frame
claim: With CSUSTA=3 and CSUINT=1 (3490-3492), TIMAX resolves to 0, 3+8*2 = 19, 3+1*2 = 5, 0, 0, indexed by SUZCNT. So the first zap runs 19 frames and the second 5. KILENE (3542-3546) only kills when SUZTIM >= CSUSTA (3) AND (SUZTIM AND CSUINT) == 0 — i.e. on every SECOND frame from frame 4 — giving at most 8 kills across the first window (which is exactly what the '8*' in TIMAX is sizing). It also takes the first live invader scanning DOWN from slot WINVMX (3548), which is slot order, not nearest-the-rim. Our ZAP_WINDOW_FIRST is 13 frames with one kill on EVERY active frame (runZapFrame, sim.ts:473-480), targeting the enemy nearest the rim. Our ZAP_WINDOW_SECOND = 5 is correct.
reasoning: 13 kills in 13 frames vs 8 kills in 19. Ours is both faster and deadlier; the arcade's staccato every-other-frame cadence is what makes the zap read as a sweep rather than a flash. Two constants plus one modulo. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:3490-3492 (CSUMAX=2, CSUINT=1, CSUSTA=3) and :3539 (TIMAX byte table) verbatim as cited. Arithmetic checks out exactly: TIMAX[1] = CSUSTA+8*(CSUINT+1) = 3+16 = 19, TIMAX[2] = 3+2 = 5. PROSUZ increments SUZCNT to 1 before the first zap and to 2 before the second, and KILENE (3548-3562) indexes TIMAX with LDX SUZCNT/CMP X,TIMAX, so the windows really are 19 and 5 frames. Decoded KILENE's gate precisely: 'CMP I,CSUSTA / IFCS / AND I,CSUINT / IFEQ' means SUZTIM>=3 AND SUZTIM even — enumerating SUZTIM=1..19 gives exactly 8 even values >=3 (4,6,...,18), which is exactly what the '8*' in TIMAX is sizing, confirming the claim's own arithmetic. EXIKIL (3562) fires for exactly one invader per KILENE call — 'LDY WINVMX / BEGIN / LDA Y,INVAY / BNE EXIKIL / DEY / MIEND' scans DOWN by fixed slot index, not by depth/nearest-rim, so the 'slot order, not nearest-the-rim' characterization is correct. Checked our side: sim.ts runZapFrame (467-480) fires nearestRimIndex on EVERY active frame while the window (ZAP_WINDOW_FIRST=13 in rules.ts:35) counts down, confirming both the frame-count and per-frame-kill divergences as described. Attacked on unit grounds — irrelevant here since this is a pure frame-count/cadence comparison, not a seconds conversion, so the 60-vs-28.44 fps rebase does not touch this finding at all. Holds completely.

title: Eight player charges may be in flight at once
claim: NPCHARG = 8 player charge slots (NICHARG = 4 more for enemy shots). FIREPC (2661-2681) scans slots NPCHARG-1..0 for a vacancy and simply does nothing if all eight are busy. Our MAX_BULLETS = 8, enforced the same way in stepFiring (sim.ts:96).
reasoning: Exact match. Our firing is also level-triggered (we fire on every frame `input.fire` is held, no rising edge), which matches FIREPC's unconditional test of the MFIRE switch bit.

title: Player charge speed is PCVELO = 9 along-units/frame = 1.143 depth/s; our BULLET_SPEED 2.4 is 2.10x too fast

ours.verbatim: export const BULLET_SPEED = 2.4       // depth units/sec (near → far); ROM rev-3 frees the slot at ~25 frames / ~0.42s

claim: MOVCHA adds PCVELO to a player charge's Y every GAME frame (2540) and retires it when it reaches ILINDDY = $F0 (2549-2554). The well is 224 units deep, so a charge crosses it in 224/9 = 24.9 frames — the frame COUNT is right — but a game frame is nine ~256 Hz IRQ ticks = 35.16 ms (256/9 = 28.44 fps, FR-001), so the crossing takes 0.875 s, not the 0.415 s originally computed. In depth units the ROM's charge speed is 9 * (256/9) / 224 = 256/224 = 1.143 depth/s. Our BULLET_SPEED = 2.4 depth/s is exactly 9 * 60/224 — the same arithmetic with the invented base — so our bullets are 2.10x too fast and cross the well in 0.417 s. The code comment's '~25 frames / ~0.42s' has the frames right and the seconds wrong.
reasoning: Originally filed CONFIRMED as 'a textbook case of the unit trap resolving to a match: 2.4 depth/s and 9 along-units/frame are the same number' — but they are only the same number if a frame is 1/60 s, so the match was manufactured by the wrong base. This is a FALSE CONFIRMED, the most dangerous defect class in the audit: the refutation pass only challenges DIVERGENCE and BOOK_WAS_WRONG findings, so this would have been printed as proof we match the arcade. Corrected for the 28.444 fps rebase (see FR-007, and FR-001, in pair-11-framerate-adjudication.json): reclassed CONFIRMED → DIVERGENCE, correct value 1.143 depth/s (= 256/224 = 8/7), true crossing time 0.875 s. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALCOMN.MAC:890 (PCVELO=9) and ALWELG.MAC MOVCHA (2530-2554: ADC I,PCVELO at 2540, CMP I,ILINDDY at 2549) and confirmed ILINDDY=$F0 at ALCOMN.MAC:819 — all verbatim as claimed. 224/9=24.89 frames to cross the well is right, and at the now-established 256/9=28.444 fps (FR-001, which this audit correctly treats as settled) that is 0.875s, not 0.417s. Converted to depth/s: 9*(256/9)/224 = 256/224 = 1.142857 depth/s. Checked one overstatement: the claim says our BULLET_SPEED=2.4 is 'exactly 9*60/224' — that product is actually 540/224=2.4107, not 2.4 exactly (our value looks instead like 60/(224/9 rounded to 25 frames) = 60/25 = 2.4, matching the code's own comment '~25 frames/~0.42s'). This is a minor imprecision in wording, not in substance: the core ratio claim (2.4 / (256/224) = 2.4*224/256 = 2.1 exactly) is exact and correct, so 'our bullets are 2.10x too fast' holds precisely. This finding is also explicitly flagged by the auditor as a corrected FALSE-CONFIRMED from the pre-rebase pass, which is the exact kind of self-aware reclassification the ground truth rules call out as legitimate. Verdict stands, with the correction that '9*60/224' is an approximation (2.41) rather than an exact restatement of 2.4.

title: The bullet/enemy hit tolerance (ENSIZE) is 7 along-units for a flipper and 6 for a fuseball; ours is roughly double
claim: COLCHK compares |charge Y - invader Y| against ENSIZE indexed by the invader's TYPE (2963-2964). ENSIZE for the fuseball is set literally to (PCVELO+3)/2 = 6 (545-546). For flippers, tankers and pulsars it is computed by TIMES8 (570-577) as ((255 - hi) + 13) >> 1 where hi is the high byte of speed*8 — which evaluates to 7 at waves 1-16 and 8 at waves 33+. In our depth units (224 along-units = depth 1.0) that is 0.031-0.036 for a flipper and 0.027 for a fuseball. Our HIT_DEPTH is 0.06 (13.4 units, ~1.9x too generous) and our FUSEBALL_HIT_DEPTH is 0.09 (20 units, ~3.3x too generous). Our comment at sim.ts:288-291 asserts the ROM's fuseball tolerance is WIDER than the default; the source shows it is NARROWER (6 vs 7).
reasoning: A hit box twice the ROM's makes every shot easier and, at BULLET_SPEED 9 units/frame, means our bullet registers a hit a frame earlier than the arcade's. The correct model is also structurally different — the tolerance is derived from the closing speed (it is half of PCVELO + the enemy's speed), so it should be a function, not a constant. [REFUTATION] Re-opened ALWELG.MAC:545-546 (fuseball ENSIZE = (PCVELO+3)/2 = 6, verbatim as cited) and TIMES8 (560-577) and decoded the macro precisely: TEMP0 sign-extends then ASL/ROL x3 multiplies the signed speed by 8; the collision-range half is computed as ((~hi)+13)>>1 where ~hi = 255-hi (one's-complement EOR $FF) and 13 = PCVELO+1+1+2. Traced the single TIMES8 call feeding ENSIZE+ZABFLI/ZABTAN/ZABPUL simultaneously (lines ~530-538: 'LDA WINVIL / JSR TIMES8 / ... STX ENSIZE+ZABFLI / STX ENSIZE+ZABTAN / STX ENSIZE+ZABPUL') confirming flipper/tanker/pulsar genuinely share one collision-range value. Pulled the actual WINVIL source table, TINVIN (ALWELG.MAC ~591-598), and computed it by hand: wave 1 WINVIL=-44, ×8=-352=0xFEA0, hi=0xFE=254, (255-254+13)>>1=7 — matches the '7 at waves 1-16' claim exactly. Wave 33 WINVIL=-108, ×8=-864=0xFCA0, hi=0xFC=252, (255-252+13)>>1=8 — matches '8 at waves 33+' exactly. Confirmed COLCHK (2932, CMP Y,ENSIZE at 2963) is the governing collision check. Checked our side at sim.ts:287-291,317 (HIT_DEPTH=0.06, FUSEBALL_HIT_DEPTH=0.09, used in resolveBulletHits) verbatim as cited, including the comment asserting the fuseball tolerance is 'wider' when 6<7/8 is actually narrower. Unit conversion is correct: 7/224=0.03125, 8/224=0.0357 (claim's '0.031-0.036' range), 6/224=0.0268 (claim's '~0.027'); 0.06*224=13.44 (claim's '13.4 units', ratio 13.4/7=1.91 ~ '1.9x'); 0.09*224=20.16 (claim's '20 units', ratio 20/6=3.36 ~ '3.3x'). Every number is exact. Structurally and numerically airtight.

title: A player charge cuts a spike down to its own position, survives two hit-frames, and scores 1 point per frame

source.verbatim: STA Y,LINEY		;YES. UPDATE LINE ENEMY TO

claim: LIFECT (2589-2626) runs from MOVCHA on every frame a player charge is at or past a spike tip: it SLOWS the charge (PCVELO - 4 = 5 units/frame, 2542-2545), sets LINEY to the charge's CURRENT Y — cutting the spike down to exactly where the bullet is — increments the charge's CHARCO counter, awards 1 point (2609-2615), and deactivates the charge once CHARCO reaches 2 (2618-2624). So one shot eats ~2 frames' worth (~10 units, ~0.045 depth) of spike and scores ~2 points, and the spike visibly recedes ahead of the bullet. Ours removes a flat SPIKE_SHORTEN = 0.08 depth, kills the bullet instantly, and awards SCORE_SPIKE_SEGMENT = 3.
reasoning: Different mechanism, different feel (the arcade's bullet burrows into the spike), and different economy: 3 points per shot vs ~2, and 0.08 depth per shot vs ~0.045. Not a headline issue, but SPIKE_SHORTEN is nearly 2x the ROM's, which — with W-039's 0.75 cap — is why our spikes are so much easier to clear. [REFUTATION] Re-opened LIFECT (ALWELG.MAC:2589-2624) and MOVCHA (2530-2554) verbatim, including the exact cited line 2602 'STA Y,LINEY ;YES. UPDATE LINE ENEMY TO'. Decoded the slow-down: MOVCHA's 'ADC I,PCVELO / LDY X,CHARCO / IFNE / SEC / SBC I,4' means the charge advances by PCVELO=9 normally but by PCVELO-4=5 once CHARCO is nonzero (i.e., after its first spike contact) — matches claim exactly. Decoded LIFECT: it fires once per frame while the charge's Y is at/past the line's tip (CMP Y,LINEY / IFCS), stores the charge's own Y into LINEY (the general-case path; a narrow edge case at CHARY>=ILINDDY zeroes it instead, immaterial here), increments CHARCO, awards exactly 1 point via UPSCORE (TEMP0=1, TEMP1=TEMP2=0), then deactivates the charge only once CHARCO reaches 2 (2618-2624, 'CMP I,2 / IFCS'). Traced through a concrete sequence: frame with CHARCO 0->1 does not deactivate; the charge then advances by only 5 (slowed) and immediately re-triggers LIFECT next frame (since LINEY was just set equal to the prior CHARY and the charge moved forward), CHARCO 1->2, deactivates same frame. So exactly two 1-point hits per bullet, confirmed, and the finding's own '~2 points'/'~10 units' hedging is appropriately cautious rather than falsely precise. Checked our side at sim.ts:333-343 (resolveSpikeHits) verbatim: instant bullet kill on first contact, flat SPIKE_SHORTEN=0.08 removed, flat SCORE_SPIKE_SEGMENT=3 awarded — exactly as claimed, a different mechanism (single flash-kill vs multi-frame burrow) with different economy (3 vs ~2 points). Holds.

title: Enemy position is an 8.8 fixed-point integer stepped once per LOGIC frame (28.4 Hz = nine ~256 Hz IRQs); ours is a float stepped by dt

ours.verbatim: e.depth = Math.max(0, Math.min(RIM_DEPTH, e.depth + e.direction * speedFor(e, ctx) * ctx.dt))

claim: JSMOVE (1731-1777) keeps each invader's position as a 16-bit fixed-point pair — INVAYL (fraction) and INVAY (integer, 0 = rim end, $F0 = far end) — and adds the per-type WINVIL/WINVIN increment once per GAME frame with a carry between the bytes. A game frame is one FRTIMR-gated MAINLN pass (ALEXEC.MAC:49-57), i.e. NINE IRQ ticks of a ~256 Hz interrupt (ALHARD.MAC:149-151) = 35.16 ms: 256/9 = 28.44 frames/second. It is NOT one IRQ, and it is not 60 Hz. Every threshold in the game ($20, $30, PULPOT, CURSY) is an integer comparison on INVAY. We carry `depth` as a float in [0,1] and integrate it as depth += speed * dt.
reasoning: The float integration itself is acceptable and is required by our frame-rate-independent core — that half of the verdict stands, and the 'accept' covers the REPRESENTATION only (8.8 fixed point vs float+dt). Corrected for the 28.444 fps rebase (see FR-001 in pair-11-framerate-adjudication.json, which calls this finding the audit's linchpin): the mapping declared here was wrong twice. The position mapping depth = ($F0 - INVAY)/224 is right, but one along-unit/frame is (256/9)/224 = 0.127 depth/s — NOT the '60/224' (0.268) asserted here and hard-coded in rules.ts — and the increment is applied once per LOGIC frame, not 'once per 60 Hz IRQ'. This finding's closing reassurance, that 'every value quoted in this audit was converted through that mapping before being compared', is therefore an indictment rather than a defence: every seconds-valued figure in the audit was 2.11x off, and each has been rebased (FR-006 through FR-017). The numeric fix belongs to FR-001/FR-008/FR-012, not here.

title: The grab line is the RIM (CURSY = $10 = depth 1.0), not a threshold — PLAYER_RIM_DEPTH = 0.92 was invented

ours.verbatim: export const PLAYER_RIM_DEPTH = 0.92  // enemy depth ≥ this on player's lane = grab

claim: There is no byte to derive the grab line from, because the kill check does not test depth. JKITST (1980-1993) reads INVAY nowhere: it tests `LDA X,INVAC1 / IFPL` (not mid-jump — W-010) and then INVAL1/INVAL2 against CURSL1/CURSL2, and calls INIPSQ. The depth gate lives one level up, in which cam program may reach the opcode: `VKITST` occurs exactly twice in ALWELG.MAC — its definition (`CAMAC JKITST,VKITST,18`, 1624) and ONE use, `KICHEK: VKITST ;TEST FOR CURSOR KILL` (2450) inside TOPPER, the CHASER cam (2447, 'CHASE PLAYER AROUND TOP'). Only a chaser can grab. CHASER (1824-1826) seats the invader on the cursor's own line — `LDA CURSY / STA X,INVAY ;PLACE EXACTLY AT TOP` — and is reached from the climb by `CMP CURSY / BEQ ATOP / IFCC` (1744-1747). CURSY is loaded from ILINLIY at every wave start (57-58) and ILINLIY = 010 (ALCOMN.MAC:820). So the grab line is (0xf0 - 0x10) / 224 = 1.0, exactly the rim. Ours was 0.92, which inverts to INVAY 33.92 — not a ROM byte — and let an invader still CLIMBING, eight along-units short of the rim, grab the player. The same constant gates resolveEnemyBoltHits, and the ROM agrees it should: the enemy charge is tested `CMP CURSY / IFCC ;AT TOP?` -> `JSR CHATOP ;YES. CHECK FOR COLLISION WITH CURSOR` (2562-2565), the same CURSY — so our bolts also killed early.
reasoning:
The cabinet already had the right number in the repo, under a different name: interpreter.ts's RIM_DEPTH = 1, whose own doc comment reads 'the ROM's CURSY, the line the cursor sits on'. One ROM constant was spelled twice, and the grab used the wrong copy — which is why no test caught it: the chaser was pinned at 1 and the grab fired at 0.92, and nothing asserted the two were the same line.

CONSEQUENCE FOR W-030 / tp1-24. With the grab line derived, it sits ABOVE the carrier's burst line ($20 = 0.9286), not below it. ATOP is tested BEFORE the carrier check (1744-1750): a carrier that actually reaches CURSY becomes a CHASER instead of bursting, so a burst can only happen strictly below the rim, and a child seated at the parent's INVAY is therefore ALWAYS born below the grab line. No split child is ever born lethal — it must climb the last ~11 frames and become a chaser first. tp1-24 ratified a difficulty change ('the children are born ABOVE the grab line, therefore a player on a flanking lane dies on the burst frame') that was an artefact of the invented 0.92; W-030's claim and REMEDIATION note are corrected accordingly. The two-threshold structure is authentic — $10 and $20 are genuinely different bytes — but their ORDER is the opposite of the one that was assumed, so the 'born lethal AND still flipping' band is empty.

Verified by re-reading Theurer's source: the provenance suite in tests/core/tp1-27.player-rim-depth.test.ts re-opens ALWELG.MAC and ALCOMN.MAC and asserts (a) ILINLIY=010, (b) JKITST's body contains no INVAY, (c) VKITST has exactly one use and it is inside TOPPER, (d) CHASER's `LDA CURSY / STA X,INVAY`.

[FIX, tp1-27] Derive it: `export const PLAYER_RIM_DEPTH = (0xf0 - 0x10) / WARP_ALONG_SPAN` (= 1.0), and derive interpreter.ts's RIM_DEPTH from that same name so the two cannot drift apart again.
